meta.description: Base layer: Open StreetMap
Base layer: Stadia Alidade Smooth Dark
Base layer: NASA Blue Marble

attribution.text: NASA via EOC Base Map Service from DLR

meta.description: Base layer: Sentinel-2 True Colour 2021

attribution.text: Sentinel-2 cloudless 2024 by EOX / Copernicus Sentinel data

meta.description: Base layer: Copernicus DEM (base layer)

Annual solar power potential, an estimate of the electricity a standard photovoltaic installation could generate over a year given local irradiance, temperature and terrain. Modelled product from the ESA GTIF Austria demonstrator.

Mapping existing solar installations shows where photovoltaic capacity is already deployed on the ground. Detected ground-mounted photovoltaic areas over a Puglia example site in southern Italy, derived from Copernicus Sentinel-2 imagery by EURAC via the APEx platform.

Cloud cover is the dominant driver of variability in solar generation. Cloud albedo from the MERRA-2 reanalysis (ISCCP), monthly mean for January 2026, with gap-free global coverage, where higher values indicate brighter, more reflective cloud cover.

Airborne aerosols reduce the solar radiation reaching photovoltaic panels. Total aerosol optical depth derived from MODIS Aqua and Terra (NASA GIBS Combined Value Added AOD), measuring the attenuation of light by airborne particles.

attribution.text: NASA GIBS / MODIS Aqua and Terra

meta.description: Surface solar radiation is the fundamental resource setting photovoltaic and solar-thermal yield. Downward shortwave radiation flux at the surface (all sky), monthly mean from NASA CERES EBAF, combining Terra and Aqua observations with reanalysis.

Mean wind power density at 100 metres hub height, the core resource metric for siting onshore turbines: it combines wind speed and air density to estimate the energy available per square metre of rotor swept area.

Wind speed is the primary determinant of wind energy yield, with power scaling roughly with the cube of wind speed. Surface wind speed from the MERRA-2 reanalysis, monthly mean for January 2026, with gap-free global coverage over land and ocean.

Wind speed over the sea surface is the primary driver of offshore wind-power potential. Monthly-mean stress-equivalent wind speed at 10 metres above the ocean from the Copernicus Marine multi-year L4 product, on a 0.25-degree gap-free global grid.

attribution.text: E.U. Copernicus Marine Service (CMEMS), Global Ocean Wind L4

meta.description: Each turbine slows the wind behind it, leaving a wake that can cut the output of turbines downwind and of neighbouring farms. Mapping wakes from Sentinel-1 radar helps plan the siting and spacing of new farms. Sentinel-1 SAR (VV) across the North Sea, early November 2025.

attribution.text: Contains modified Copernicus Sentinel-1 data 2025, mosaicked via Microsoft Planetary Computer

meta.description: Wave height sets the offshore wave-energy resource and the structural loads acting on offshore wind foundations. Significant wave height (Hm0) from the Copernicus Marine global ocean waves analysis and forecast, at 0.083 degree resolution.

attribution.text: Copernicus Marine Service / Mercator Ocean International

meta.description: Longer-period waves carry more energy, so wave period is a key factor in wave energy potential. Wave energy period (Tm-10) from the Copernicus Marine global ocean waves analysis and forecast.

attribution.text: Copernicus Marine Service / Mercator Ocean International

meta.description: Water depth determines whether offshore wind uses fixed-bottom or floating foundations and bounds the feasible installation zone. Mean sea-floor depth for European seas, 2022 release from the EMODnet Bathymetry Consortium.
The coastline defines the shoreward boundary for nearshore offshore wind and marine energy siting. Reference coastline product from EMODnet Bathymetry.

Surface water and seasonal wetness mark reservoir extent, river systems and saturated ground relevant to hydropower resource. Copernicus Land High Resolution Layer Water and Wetness 2015, at 10-metre resolution across Europe, mapping permanent and temporary water and wet surfaces.

attribution.text: Copernicus Land Monitoring Service (CLMS), Water & Wetness HRL 2015 / EEA

meta.description: Recent precipitation is the water arriving in a catchment. Observed precipitation from Copernicus EMS / EFAS, feeding the rivers and reservoirs that drive hydropower generation.

Snowpack is the water that fills hydropower reservoirs through spring melt, making it a key driver of seasonal generation. Snow water equivalent from Copernicus EMS, where values represent the depth of water held in the snowpack.

Snowpack relative to normal signals whether spring melt will bring above- or below-average inflow. Ten-day snow anomaly from Copernicus EMS / EFAS, the departure of current snow water equivalent from the seasonal average.

Soil moisture governs how much rainfall becomes river runoff, shaping the water available for hydropower generation and signalling drought stress on supply. Soil moisture from Copernicus EMS / EFAS, indicating how saturated the ground is across Europe.

How wet the ground is relative to normal sets how much of the next rainfall becomes runoff and reservoir inflow. Soil moisture anomaly from Copernicus EMS / EFAS, the departure of current soil moisture from the seasonal average.
The river network along which runoff concentrates toward hydropower reservoirs. Major rivers from the Copernicus EMS / EFAS LISFLOOD drainage network.
Catchments are the drainage basins that feed each river and reservoir, the unit hydropower operators plan in. Catchment boundaries from Copernicus EMS / EFAS.

The high-voltage transmission grid is the backbone that moves power from generation to demand and connects countries, so it shapes where new energy projects can connect. European transmission lines (220 to 750 kV AC and all HVDC), coloured by voltage.

attribution.text: © OpenStreetMap contributors (ODbL), processed by PyPSA-Eur / TU Berlin

meta.description: Areas that burn frequently pose a recurring threat to transmission lines, substations, and other energy infrastructure. Number of times each location was mapped as burned area by MODIS over the period from 2000 to the present, from the Global Wildfire Information System (GWIS).

Active fires near overhead lines and substations can trip circuits and force pre-emptive shutdowns, making real-time fire detection valuable for grid operators. Active fire detection from EUMETSAT, derived from the Meteosat SEVIRI 3.9 µm channel, flagging pixels with potential or active fire across Europe and Africa.

Flooding is a direct physical threat to power plants, substations and transmission infrastructure sited near rivers. Combined river-flood hazard scenarios from Copernicus EMS / EFAS, mapping the areas across Europe exposed to flooding at low, medium and high severity (return-period) levels.

How likely a damaging flood is at a given location shapes the risk to energy assets and the need for operational mitigation. Flood Probability shows the likelihood that river flow will exceed a given flood severity level, based on Copernicus EMS / EFAS ensemble forecasts, where flood severity reflects return periods (2-year frequent, 5-year moderate, 20-year severe).

When a flood strikes, operators need to know immediately which energy assets are inundated. Rapid Flood Mapping delivers satellite-derived flood extent from Copernicus EMS / EFAS for active flood events, supporting emergency response and damage assessment for energy infrastructure.

Built-up surfaces mark where energy demand concentrates and constrain where new infrastructure can be sited. Copernicus High Resolution Layer Imperviousness Density 2015, mapping the degree of soil sealing from built-up and artificial surfaces across Europe.

Nitrogen dioxide is produced by high-temperature combustion, so it traces fossil-fuel power plants, heavy industry, shipping lanes and road traffic. Tropospheric NO2 column from Copernicus Sentinel-5P TROPOMI, yearly mean processed by S5P-PAL, where higher values mark concentrated combustion activity such as coal-power clusters and industrial regions.

attribution.text: Copernicus Sentinel-5P (TROPOMI) / S5P-PAL, processed by S&T, hosted by ESA SEF

meta.description: Methane is the main component of natural gas, so mapping atmospheric methane reveals enhancements over oil and gas fields and other major emitters. Column-averaged dry-air methane (XCH4) from Copernicus Sentinel-5P TROPOMI, yearly mean processed by S5P-PAL, where higher values flag regions of elevated methane such as super-emitting oil and gas basins.

attribution.text: Copernicus Sentinel-5P (TROPOMI) / S5P-PAL, processed by S&T, hosted by ESA SEF

meta.description: Land cover identifies which areas are suitable or constrained for renewable energy development. CORINE Land Cover 2018 from the Copernicus Land Monitoring Service, a 44-class European land cover and land use inventory.

attribution.text: CORINE Land Cover 2018, Copernicus Land Monitoring Service / EEA

meta.description: Terrain elevation shapes wind exposure on ridgelines and constrains where energy infrastructure and hydropower can be built. Shaded-relief terrain layer from EOX, derived from a blend of public digital elevation models including GMTED2010 and EU-DEM.

A true-colour view of the land surface supports visual reconnaissance of candidate wind and solar sites. Sentinel-2 cloudless mosaic for 2025 from EOX, a near-cloud-free composite of Copernicus Sentinel-2 imagery.

attribution.text: Sentinel-2 cloudless 2025 by EOX / Copernicus Sentinel data
